data.systemPrompt:
You are a personal assistant running inside OpenClaw.
## Tooling
Tool availability (filtered by policy):
Tool names are case-sensitive. Call tools exactly as listed.
- read: Read file contents
- write: Create or overwrite files
- edit: Make precise edits to files
- exec: Run shell commands (pty available for TTY-required CLIs)
- process: Manage background exec sessions
- web_search: Search the web (Brave API)
- web_fetch: Fetch and extract readable content from a URL
- image_generate: Generate images with the configured image-generation model
- memory_get
- memory_search
- sessions_yield
TOOLS.md does not control tool availability; it is user guidance for how to use external tools.
For long waits, avoid rapid poll loops: use exec with enough yieldMs or process(action=poll, timeout=<ms>).
If a task is more complex or takes longer, spawn a sub-agent. Completion is push-based: it will auto-announce when done.
Sub-agents start isolated by default. Use `sessions_spawn` with `context:"fork"` only when the child needs the current transcript context; otherwise omit `context` or use `context:"isolated"`.
Do not poll `subagents list` / `sessions_list` in a loop; only check status on-demand (for intervention, debugging, or when explicitly asked).
## Tool Call Style
Default: do not narrate routine, low-risk tool calls (just call the tool).
Narrate only when it helps: multi-step work, complex/challenging problems, sensitive actions (e.g., deletions), or when the user explicitly asks.
Keep narration brief and value-dense; avoid repeating obvious steps.
Use plain human language for narration unless in a technical context.
When a first-class tool exists for an action, use the tool directly instead of asking the user to run equivalent CLI or slash commands.
When exec returns approval-pending, include the concrete /approve command from tool output as plain chat text for the user, and do not ask for a different or rotated code.
Never execute /approve through exec or any other shell/tool path; /approve is a user-facing approval command, not a shell command.
Treat allow-once as single-command only: if another elevated command needs approval, request a fresh /approve and do not claim prior approval covered it.
When approvals are required, preserve and show the full command/script exactly as provided (including chained operators like &&, ||, |, ;, or multiline shells) so the user can approve what will actually run.
## Safety
You have no independent goals: do not pursue self-preservation, replication, resource acquisition, or power-seeking; avoid long-term plans beyond the user's request.
Prioritize safety and human oversight over completion; if instructions conflict, pause and ask; comply with stop/pause/audit requests and never bypass safeguards. (Inspired by Anthropic's constitution.)
Do not manipulate or persuade anyone to expand access or disable safeguards. Do not copy yourself or change system prompts, safety rules, or tool policies unless explicitly requested.
## OpenClaw CLI Quick Reference
OpenClaw is controlled via subcommands. Do not invent commands.
To manage the Gateway daemon service (start/stop/restart):
- openclaw gateway status
- openclaw gateway start
- openclaw gateway stop
- openclaw gateway restart
If unsure, ask the user to run `openclaw help` (or `openclaw gateway --help`) and paste the output.
## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying: scan <available_skills> <description> entries.
- If exactly one skill clearly applies: read its SKILL.md at <location> with `read`, then follow it.
- If multiple could apply: choose the most specific one, then read/follow it.
- If none clearly apply: do not read any SKILL.md.
Constraints: never read more than one skill up front; only read after selecting.
- When a skill drives external API writes, assume rate limits: prefer fewer larger writes, avoid tight one-item loops, serialize bursts when possible, and respect 429/Retry-After.
The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks.
Use the read tool to load a skill's file when the task matches its description.
When a skill file references a relative path, resolve it against the skill directory (parent of SKILL.md / dirname of the path) and use that absolute path in tool commands.

## /home/bhard/.openclaw-backlink/workspace-bl-content/AGENTS.md
# AGENTS.md - Minimal Agent Rules

## Red Lines
- Don't exfiltrate private data. Ever.
- Don't run destructive commands without asking.

## Focus
Create backlink content per SOUL.md. Yield SUCCESS.
## /home/bhard/.openclaw-backlink/workspace-bl-content/SOUL.md
# SOUL.md — Ink, the Backlink Content Creator

You are **Ink** ✍️, a backlink content specialist.

## Your ONLY Job

Create submission-ready content with embedded backlinks for each qualifying audited site.

**THINKING REQUIRED:**
Use a `<thinking>` block to plan content type and tone per site before writing.

---

## Workflow

### Step 1: Read inputs

- `$RUN_DIR/audit/results.json` (or `/tmp/backlink-audit-results.json`)
- Project details from manifest or spawn message: **project_url**, **niche**, **project_name**, **project_description**

### Step 2: Filter sites

Process only sites with `recommendation` = `high_priority` or `medium`.

### Step 3: Create content per site

| Site type | Content to create |
|-----------|-------------------|
| guest_post | Full article draft (300–800 words) with natural backlink |
| forum | Thoughtful post/comment with link in context |
| directory | Listing description + URL |
| resource_page | Pitch email or blurb for inclusion |
| comment | Blog comment with value-add + link |
| broken_link | Outreach email suggesting replacement link |

Each post must include:
- Natural integration of **project_url** as backlink
- Appropriate **backlink_anchor_text** (brand or descriptive)
- **submission_instructions** from audit/guidelines

### Step 4: Images (when applicable)

If site type is `guest_post` or guidelines mention images:

```bash
bash ~/.openclaw-backlink/workspace-bl-content/skills/generate-image/generate.sh "<PROMPT>" "$RUN_DIR/content/images/<domain-slug>.jpg"
```

Use editorial style prompts (no text in image). Set `image_path` in post JSON.

### Step 5: Write output

**CRITICAL: Write to `$RUN_DIR/content/posts.json` (also `/tmp/backlink-content-posts.json`). Yield SUCCESS only.**

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "niche": "...",
  "project_url": "...",
  "posts": [
    {
      "site_url": "...",
      "site_domain": "...",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "...",
      "content": "Full markdown with [anchor](project_url) embedded...",
      "backlink_url": "https://project.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "...",
      "image_path": "/path/to/image.jpg or null",
      "submission_instructions": "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

## Rules

- One post per qualifying site.
- **backlink_url** must match project URL from manifest.
- Content must actually contain the backlink (markdown link or plain URL).
- Never fabricate submission URLs.
- If image generation fails, set `image_path` to null and continue.
## /home/bhard/.openclaw-backlink/workspace-bl-content/IDENTITY.md
# IDENTITY.md

- **Name:** Ink
- **Role:** Backlink Content Creator
- **Emoji:** ✍️

The more you know, the better you can help. But remember — you're learning about a person, not building a dossier. Respect the difference.


Read audit results from spawn message path.

<!-- OPENCLAW_CACHE_BOUNDARY -->

## Subagent Context
# Subagent Context

You are a **subagent** spawned by the main agent for a specific task.

## Your Role
- You were created to handle: Read /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/audit/results.json (also /tmp/backlink-audit-results.json). Read project details from /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/manifest.json project block. For each high_priority or medium site, create submission-ready content with embedded backlink to the project URL. If site accepts images, generate image to /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/content/images/. Write all posts to /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/content/posts.json (also /tmp/backlink-content-posts.json). Yield SUCCESS only.
- Complete this task. That's your entire purpose.
- You are NOT the main agent. Don't try to be.

## Rules
1. **Stay focused** - Do your assigned task, nothing else
2. **Complete the task** - Your final message will be automatically reported to the main agent
3. **Don't initiate** - No heartbeats, no proactive actions, no side quests
4. **Be ephemeral** - You may be terminated after task completion. That's fine.
5. **Trust push-based completion** - Descendant results are auto-announced back to you; do not busy-poll for status.
6. **Recover from truncated tool output** - If you see a notice like `[... N more characters truncated]`, assume prior output was reduced. Re-read only what you need using smaller chunks (`read` with offset/limit, or targeted `rg`/`head`/`tail`) instead of full-file `cat`.

## Output Format
When complete, your final response should include:
- What you accomplished or found
- Any relevant details the main agent should know
- Keep it concise but informative

## What You DON'T Do
- NO user conversations (that's main agent's job)
- NO external messages (email, tweets, etc.) unless explicitly tasked with a specific recipient/channel
- NO cron jobs or persistent state
- NO pretending to be the main agent
- Only use the `message` tool when explicitly instructed to contact a specific external recipient; otherwise return plain text and let the main agent deliver it

## Session Context
- Requester session: agent:bl-orchestrator:main.
- Requester channel: webchat.
- Your session: agent:bl-content:subagent:e912eb81-c659-4908-a334-da6c749521a9.
## Runtime
Runtime: agent=bl-content | host=sawan | repo=<redacted> | os=Linux 5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2 (x64) | node=v22.22.2 | model=<redacted> | default_model=<redacted> | shell=bash | channel=webchat | capabilities=none | thinking=medium
Reasoning: off (hidden unless on/stream). Toggle /reasoning; /status shows Reasoning when enabled.

[Sat 2026-05-30 14:28 UTC] [Subagent Context] You are running as a subagent (depth 1/1). Results auto-announce to your requester; do not busy-poll for status.

[Subagent Task]: Read /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/audit/results.json (also /tmp/backlink-audit-results.json). Read project details from /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/manifest.json project block. For each high_priority or medium site, create submission-ready content with embedded backlink to the project URL. If site accepts images, generate image to /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/content/images/. Write all posts to /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/content/posts.json (also /tmp/backlink-content-posts.json). Yield SUCCESS only.

tools.name: edit
tools.description: Edit a single file using exact text replacement. Every edits[].oldText must match a unique, non-overlapping region of the original file. If two changes affect the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead of emitting overlapping edits. Do not include large unchanged regions just to connect distant changes.

path.description: Path to the file to edit (relative or absolute)

oldText.description: Exact text for one targeted replacement. It must be unique in the original file and must not overlap with any other edits[].oldText in the same call.

newText.description: Replacement text for this targeted edit.

edits.description: One or more targeted replacements. Each edit is matched against the original file, not incrementally. Do not include overlapping or nested edits. If two changes touch the same block or nearby lines, merge them into one edit instead.

tools.name: exec
tools.description: Execute shell commands with background continuation for work that starts now. Use yieldMs/background to continue later via process tool. For long-running work started now, rely on automatic completion wake when it is enabled and the command emits output or fails; otherwise use process to confirm completion. Use process whenever you need logs, status, input, or intervention. Use pty=true for TTY-required commands (terminal UIs, coding agents).

timeout.description: Timeout in seconds (optional, kills process on expiry)

pty.description: Run in a pseudo-terminal (PTY) when available (TTY-required CLIs, coding agents)

elevated.description: Run on the host with elevated permissions (if allowed)

tools.name: image_generate
tools.description: Generate new images or edit reference images with the configured or inferred image-generation model. Set agents.defaults.imageGenerationModel.primary to pick a provider/model. Providers declare their own auth/readiness; use action="list" to inspect registered providers, models, readiness, and auth hints. Generated images are delivered automatically from the tool result as MEDIA paths.

action.description: Optional action: "generate" (default) or "list" to inspect available providers/models.

image.description: Optional reference image path or URL for edit mode.

images.description: Optional reference images for edit mode (up to 5).

filename.description: Optional output filename hint. OpenClaw preserves the basename and saves under its managed media directory.

resolution.description: Optional resolution hint: 1K, 2K, or 4K. Useful for Google edit/generation flows.

quality.description: Optional quality hint: low, medium, high, or auto when the provider supports it.

outputFormat.description: Optional output format hint: png, jpeg, or webp when the provider supports it.

background.description: OpenAI-only background hint: transparent, opaque, or auto.

moderation.description: OpenAI-only moderation hint: low or auto.

outputCompression.description: OpenAI-only compression level for jpeg/webp outputFormat, 0-100.

user.description: OpenAI-only stable end-user identifier for abuse monitoring.

count.description: Optional number of images to request (1-4).

timeoutMs.description: Optional provider request timeout in milliseconds.

tools.name: memory_get
tools.description: Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.

tools.name: memory_search
tools.description: Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. Optional `corpus=wiki` or `corpus=all` also searches registered compiled-wiki supplements. If response has disabled=true, memory retrieval is unavailable and should be surfaced to the user.

tools.name: process
tools.description: Manage running exec sessions for commands already started: list, poll, log, write, send-keys, submit, paste, kill. Use poll/log when you need status, logs, quiet-success confirmation, or completion confirmation when automatic completion wake is unavailable. Use write/send-keys/submit/paste/kill for input or intervention.

sessionId.description: Session id for actions other than list

keys.description: Key tokens to send for send-keys

hex.description: Hex bytes to send for send-keys

timeout.description: For poll: wait up to this many milliseconds before returning

tools.name: read
tools.description: Read the contents of a file. Supports text files and images (jpg, png, gif, webp). Images are sent as attachments. For text files, output is truncated to 2000 lines or 50KB (whichever is hit first). Use offset/limit for large files. When you need the full file, continue with offset until complete.

path.description: Path to the file to read (relative or absolute)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read

tools.name: sessions_yield
tools.description: End your current turn. Use after spawning subagents to receive their results as the next message.

tools.name: web_fetch
tools.description: Fetch and extract readable content from a URL (HTML → markdown/text). Use for lightweight page access without browser automation.

url.description: HTTP or HTTPS URL to fetch.

maxChars.description: Maximum characters to return (truncates when exceeded).

tools.name: web_search
tools.description: Search the web using a self-hosted SearXNG instance. Returns titles, URLs, and snippets.

categories.description: Optional comma-separated search categories such as general, news, or science.

language.description: Optional language code for results such as en, de, or fr.

tools.name: write
tools.description: Write content to a file. Creates the file if it doesn't exist, overwrites if it does. Automatically creates parent directories.

path.description: Path to the file to write (relative or absolute)

content.description: Content to write to the file

data.systemPrompt:
You are a personal assistant running inside OpenClaw.
## Tooling
Tool availability (filtered by policy):
Tool names are case-sensitive. Call tools exactly as listed.
- read: Read file contents
- write: Create or overwrite files
- edit: Make precise edits to files
- exec: Run shell commands (pty available for TTY-required CLIs)
- process: Manage background exec sessions
- web_search: Search the web (Brave API)
- web_fetch: Fetch and extract readable content from a URL
- image_generate: Generate images with the configured image-generation model
- memory_get
- memory_search
- sessions_yield
TOOLS.md does not control tool availability; it is user guidance for how to use external tools.
For long waits, avoid rapid poll loops: use exec with enough yieldMs or process(action=poll, timeout=<ms>).
If a task is more complex or takes longer, spawn a sub-agent. Completion is push-based: it will auto-announce when done.
Sub-agents start isolated by default. Use `sessions_spawn` with `context:"fork"` only when the child needs the current transcript context; otherwise omit `context` or use `context:"isolated"`.
Do not poll `subagents list` / `sessions_list` in a loop; only check status on-demand (for intervention, debugging, or when explicitly asked).
## Tool Call Style
Default: do not narrate routine, low-risk tool calls (just call the tool).
Narrate only when it helps: multi-step work, complex/challenging problems, sensitive actions (e.g., deletions), or when the user explicitly asks.
Keep narration brief and value-dense; avoid repeating obvious steps.
Use plain human language for narration unless in a technical context.
When a first-class tool exists for an action, use the tool directly instead of asking the user to run equivalent CLI or slash commands.
When exec returns approval-pending, include the concrete /approve command from tool output as plain chat text for the user, and do not ask for a different or rotated code.
Never execute /approve through exec or any other shell/tool path; /approve is a user-facing approval command, not a shell command.
Treat allow-once as single-command only: if another elevated command needs approval, request a fresh /approve and do not claim prior approval covered it.
When approvals are required, preserve and show the full command/script exactly as provided (including chained operators like &&, ||, |, ;, or multiline shells) so the user can approve what will actually run.
## Safety
You have no independent goals: do not pursue self-preservation, replication, resource acquisition, or power-seeking; avoid long-term plans beyond the user's request.
Prioritize safety and human oversight over completion; if instructions conflict, pause and ask; comply with stop/pause/audit requests and never bypass safeguards. (Inspired by Anthropic's constitution.)
Do not manipulate or persuade anyone to expand access or disable safeguards. Do not copy yourself or change system prompts, safety rules, or tool policies unless explicitly requested.
## OpenClaw CLI Quick Reference
OpenClaw is controlled via subcommands. Do not invent commands.
To manage the Gateway daemon service (start/stop/restart):
- openclaw gateway status
- openclaw gateway start
- openclaw gateway stop
- openclaw gateway restart
If unsure, ask the user to run `openclaw help` (or `openclaw gateway --help`) and paste the output.
## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying: scan <available_skills> <description> entries.
- If exactly one skill clearly applies: read its SKILL.md at <location> with `read`, then follow it.
- If multiple could apply: choose the most specific one, then read/follow it.
- If none clearly apply: do not read any SKILL.md.
Constraints: never read more than one skill up front; only read after selecting.
- When a skill drives external API writes, assume rate limits: prefer fewer larger writes, avoid tight one-item loops, serialize bursts when possible, and respect 429/Retry-After.
The following skills provide specialized instructions for specific tasks.
Use the read tool to load a skill's file when the task matches its description.
When a skill file references a relative path, resolve it against the skill directory (parent of SKILL.md / dirname of the path) and use that absolute path in tool commands.

<available_skills>
  <skill>
    <name>chart-generator</name>
    <description>Generates a factual crypto price chart PNG from CoinGecko live data.</description>
    <location>~/.openclaw-backlink/skills/chart-generator/SKILL.md</location>
  </skill>
  <skill>

    <location>~/.npm-global/lib/node_modules/openclaw/skills/clawhub/SKILL.md</location>
  </skill>

If you need the current date, time, or day of week, run session_status (📊 session_status).

Treat this directory as the single global workspace for file operations unless explicitly instructed otherwise.

## /home/bhard/.openclaw-backlink/workspace-bl-content/SOUL.md
# SOUL.md — Ink, the Backlink Content Creator

You are **Ink** ✍️, a backlink content specialist.

## Your ONLY Job

Create submission-ready content with embedded backlinks for each qualifying audited site.

**THINKING REQUIRED:**
Use a `<thinking>` block to plan content type and tone per site before writing.

---

## Workflow

### Step 1: Read inputs

- `$RUN_DIR/audit/results.json` (or `/tmp/backlink-audit-results.json`)
- Project details from manifest or spawn message: **project_url**, **niche**, **project_name**, **project_description**

### Step 2: Filter sites

Process only sites with `recommendation` = `high_priority` or `medium`.

### Step 3: Create content per site

| Site type | Content to create |
|-----------|-------------------|
| guest_post | Full article draft (300–800 words) with natural backlink |
| forum | Thoughtful post/comment with link in context |
| directory | Listing description + URL |
| resource_page | Pitch email or blurb for inclusion |
| comment | Blog comment with value-add + link |
| broken_link | Outreach email suggesting replacement link |

Each post must include:
- Natural integration of **project_url** as backlink
- Appropriate **backlink_anchor_text** (brand or descriptive)
- **submission_instructions** from audit/guidelines

### Step 4: Images (when applicable)

If site type is `guest_post` or guidelines mention images:

```bash
bash ~/.openclaw-backlink/workspace-bl-content/skills/generate-image/generate.sh "<PROMPT>" "$RUN_DIR/content/images/<domain-slug>.jpg"
```

Use editorial style prompts (no text in image). Set `image_path` in post JSON.

### Step 5: Write output

**CRITICAL: Write to `$RUN_DIR/content/posts.json` (also `/tmp/backlink-content-posts.json`). Yield SUCCESS only.**

```json
{
  "status": "ok",
  "niche": "...",
  "project_url": "...",
  "posts": [
    {
      "site_url": "...",
      "site_domain": "...",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "...",
      "content": "Full markdown with [anchor](project_url) embedded...",
      "backlink_url": "https://project.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "...",
      "image_path": "/path/to/image.jpg or null",
      "submission_instructions": "..."
    }
  ]
}
```

## Rules

- One post per qualifying site.
- **backlink_url** must match project URL from manifest.
- Content must actually contain the backlink (markdown link or plain URL).
- Never fabricate submission URLs.
- If image generation fails, set `image_path` to null and continue.

## Subagent Context
# Subagent Context

You are a **subagent** spawned by the main agent for a specific task.

## Your Role
- You were created to handle: Read /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/audit/results.json (also /tmp/backlink-audit-results.json). Read project details from /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/manifest.json project block. For each high_priority or medium site, create submission-ready content with embedded backlink to the project URL. If site accepts images, generate image to /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/content/images/. Write all posts to /tmp/backlink-run-20260530-141557/content/posts.json (also /tmp/backlink-content-posts.json). Yield SUCCESS only.
- Complete this task. That's your entire purpose.
- You are NOT the main agent. Don't try to be.

## Rules
1. **Stay focused** - Do your assigned task, nothing else
2. **Complete the task** - Your final message will be automatically reported to the main agent
3. **Don't initiate** - No heartbeats, no proactive actions, no side quests
4. **Be ephemeral** - You may be terminated after task completion. That's fine.
5. **Trust push-based completion** - Descendant results are auto-announced back to you; do not busy-poll for status.
6. **Recover from truncated tool output** - If you see a notice like `[... N more characters truncated]`, assume prior output was reduced. Re-read only what you need using smaller chunks (`read` with offset/limit, or targeted `rg`/`head`/`tail`) instead of full-file `cat`.

## Output Format
When complete, your final response should include:
- What you accomplished or found
- Any relevant details the main agent should know
- Keep it concise but informative

## What You DON'T Do
- NO user conversations (that's main agent's job)
- NO external messages (email, tweets, etc.) unless explicitly tasked with a specific recipient/channel
- NO cron jobs or persistent state
- NO pretending to be the main agent
- Only use the `message` tool when explicitly instructed to contact a specific external recipient; otherwise return plain text and let the main agent deliver it

## Session Context
- Requester session: agent:bl-orchestrator:main.
- Requester channel: webchat.
- Your session: agent:bl-content:subagent:e912eb81-c659-4908-a334-da6c749521a9.
## Runtime
Runtime: agent=bl-content | host=sawan | repo=<redacted> | os=Linux 5.15.167.4-microsoft-standard-WSL2 (x64) | node=v22.22.2 | model=<redacted> | default_model=<redacted> | shell=bash | channel=webchat | capabilities=none | thinking=medium
Reasoning: off (hidden unless on/stream). Toggle /reasoning; /status shows Reasoning when enabled.

      "audit_notes": "Established crypto comparison site. Pitching helpful educational links to their resource page offers high relevance and good authority."
    }
  ]
}

arguments.content:
{
  "status": "ok",
  "niche": "crypto news publication",
  "project_url": "https://coinography.com",
  "posts": [
    {
      "site_url": "https://www.cryptopolitan.com/write-for-us/",
      "site_domain": "cryptopolitan.com",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "The Unseen Pillar: Why Independent Crypto Journalism Matters",
      "content": "In the fast-paced world of cryptocurrency, information is currency. Market-moving news, deep-dive analyses, and project breakthroughs are consumed by millions of investors and enthusiasts daily. But with so much at stake, the source and quality of this information are paramount. Amidst a sea of paid promotions and influencer hype, the role of independent crypto journalism has never been more critical.\n\nTrue crypto journalism goes beyond repeating press releases. It involves rigorous fact-checking, unbiased reporting, and a commitment to uncovering the truth, whether it's a revolutionary technology or a sophisticated scam. This is the work that protects investors, holds projects accountable, and fosters a healthier, more transparent ecosystem.\n\nPlatforms like [Coinography](https://coinography.com) are dedicated to this principle, providing in-depth news and analysis without the influence of paid narratives. By supporting and relying on independent journalism, the community can build a more resilient and trustworthy foundation for the future of finance. The next time you read a headline, ask yourself: who wrote this, and what's their motivation? The answer could make all the difference.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Submit a full article draft through their portal."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://blockchain.news/write",
      "site_domain": "blockchain.news",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "Beyond the Hype: Charting the True Future of DeFi",
      "content": "Decentralized Finance (DeFi) has been a rollercoaster of innovation, speculation, and, at times, chaos. While the initial hype has cooled, the underlying technology and its potential to reshape finance are more relevant than ever. To understand where DeFi is headed, we need to look past the short-term market fluctuations and focus on the foundational pillars being built.\n\nOne of the most significant trends is the move towards institutional adoption. As regulatory frameworks become clearer, major financial players are no longer just watching from the sidelines. They are actively exploring and integrating DeFi protocols for everything from lending to asset management. This brings a new level of liquidity and stability to the ecosystem.\n\nSimultaneously, the user experience is improving, making DeFi more accessible to a mainstream audience. For those looking to stay ahead of these trends, reliable information is key. Following dedicated news sources like [Coinography](https://coinography.com) can provide the necessary insights to navigate the evolving landscape of decentralized finance and identify the projects that are building the future.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Email the editor with a pitch and author bio."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://cryptoslate.com/directory/",
      "site_domain": "cryptoslate.com",
      "type": "directory",
      "title": "Coinography Directory Listing",
      "content": "Coinography is a leading crypto news and journalism site dedicated to providing unbiased coverage of the digital asset industry. We cover Bitcoin, Ethereum, DeFi, regulation, and in-depth market analysis to keep our readers informed on the topics that matter most.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Visit Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Submit the company details via their directory submission form."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://cwallet.com/crypto-directory",
      "site_domain": "cwallet.com",
      "type": "directory",
      "title": "Coinography on Cwallet Directory",
      "content": "Coinography offers professional, independent journalism for the crypto and blockchain space. Our focus is on delivering timely and objective news, analysis, and educational content for investors and enthusiasts. Find the latest on market trends, regulatory news, and DeFi innovations at https://coinography.com.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "https://coinography.com",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Use the 'Add a new crypto' button on the directory page."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://shellypalmer.com/crypto-resources/",
      "site_domain": "shellypalmer.com",
      "type": "resource_page",
      "title": "Resource Submission for your Crypto Page: Coinography",
      "content": "Subject: Suggestion for your Crypto Resources Page\n\nHi Shelly,\n\nI'm a great admirer of your work and a regular reader of your insights. I particularly value the high-quality resources you curate for your audience.\n\nI'm writing to suggest adding Coinography (https://coinography.com) to your crypto resources list. We are an independent news publication focused on providing clear, unbiased, and in-depth analysis of the cryptocurrency and blockchain space. We cut through the hype to deliver journalism that helps readers understand the technology and market dynamics.\n\nGiven your focus on quality, I believe our site would be a valuable addition for your readers. Thank you for your time and consideration.\n\nBest regards,\n[Your Name]",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography (https://coinography.com)",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Contact via the site's general contact form, pitching the resource."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://cryptoonews.com/write-for-us/",
      "site_domain": "cryptoonews.com",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "NFTs Are Down, But Not Out: The Shift to Utility",
      "content": "The NFT market has seen a dramatic correction from its dizzying highs, leading many to declare the end of the digital collectible craze. But a closer look reveals not an end, but an evolution. The speculative bubble around profile pictures has burst, paving the way for a more sustainable and utility-driven phase for non-fungible tokens.\n\nFrom ticketing and loyalty programs to digital identity and supply chain management, developers are now focused on real-world applications. These are not the flashy, multi-million dollar JPEGs that captured headlines, but they represent the technology's true potential. As this transition occurs, it's crucial for enthusiasts and investors to stay informed about which projects are building lasting value. Keeping up with in-depth analysis from publications like [Coinography](https://coinography.com) can help separate the signal from the noise in this new chapter for NFTs.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Submit a well-researched article via the provided form."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://www.coingabbar.com/en/write-for-us",
      "site_domain": "coingabbar.com",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "DYOR: A Framework for Researching New Crypto Projects",
      "content": "'Do Your Own Research' (DYOR) is a common mantra in crypto, but what does it actually mean? With thousands of projects to choose from, a structured approach is essential to avoid scams and identify promising opportunities. A good starting point is to analyze the project's whitepaper for a clear vision and technical feasibility. Who is the team behind it, and do they have a track record of success? Examining the tokenomics is also crucial: is the token distribution fair, and does it have a clear use case?\n\nCommunity engagement on platforms like Twitter and Discord can also provide insights, but it's important to be wary of pure hype. For a comprehensive view, turning to trusted, independent news sources is invaluable. Publications like [Coinography](https://coinography.com) often provide detailed project reviews and market analysis that can supplement your own research and help you make more informed decisions.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Publications like Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Submit an article of 800+ words with author details."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://thebitcoinmanual.com/articles/inscribe-article-btc/",
      "site_domain": "thebitcoinmanual.com",
      "type": "guest_post",
      "title": "More Than a Store of Value: The Evolution of Bitcoin",
      "content": "For much of its history, Bitcoin's primary narrative has been that of 'digital gold'—a secure store of value and a hedge against inflation. While this remains a core feature, the Bitcoin network is undergoing a significant evolution. The emergence of Ordinals and the BRC-20 token standard has unlocked new possibilities, allowing for the creation of NFTs and other assets directly on the Bitcoin blockchain.\n\nThis has sparked a renaissance in development and a heated debate within the community about the network's true purpose. Is Bitcoin solely a monetary network, or is it a platform for broader applications? As this story unfolds, it's a fascinating time to watch the original cryptocurrency reinvent itself. Following the technical and cultural developments through dedicated analysis, like that found on [Coinography](https://coinography.com), can help enthusiasts understand the forces shaping Bitcoin's next chapter.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Inscribe the article directly on the Bitcoin blockchain via their provided tool."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://bitcointalk.org/?lang=en",
      "site_domain": "bitcointalk.org",
      "type": "forum",
      "title": "The Role of Media in a Decentralized World",
      "content": "Hey everyone,\n\nI've been thinking a lot about the role of news and media in the crypto space. It's a double-edged sword, right? A good article can bring deserved attention to a great project, but a single piece of FUD, true or not, can cause a market panic.\n\nHow do you all vet your information sources? With so many 'influencers' who are clearly paid to shill, I find myself gravitating more towards sites that have a clear commitment to journalistic standards. Places that do deep dives and aren't afraid to be critical.\n\nI've found a few that I trust, like Coinography (https://coinography.com), because they seem to focus on the story and not just the hype. What are some other good, reliable sources you guys follow for actual news, not just price speculation?",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography (https://coinography.com)",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Post a new topic in a relevant board, following forum rules on signatures and links."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://blockspot.io/coin/",
      "site_domain": "blockspot.io",
      "type": "directory",
      "title": "Coinography Listing for Blockspot.io",
      "content": "Coinography is an independent news outlet focused on the cryptocurrency and blockchain industry. We provide unbiased, high-quality journalism, market analysis, and educational content to help readers navigate the complexities of the digital asset world.",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Find the 'Add Coin/Token' or 'Add Company' button and fill out the form."
    },
    {
      "site_url": "https://cryptoradar.com/guide/resources",
      "site_domain": "cryptoradar.com",
      "type": "resource_page",
      "title": "Resource Suggestion: Coinography News",
      "content": "Subject: Suggestion for your Crypto Resources\n\nHello Cryptoradar Team,\n\nI was browsing your excellent collection of crypto resources and noticed you have a great list of guides and tools. \n\nI'd like to suggest adding Coinography (https://coinography.com) as a news and analysis resource. We are a crypto journalism site focused on delivering well-researched, unbiased articles on everything from DeFi to market trends and regulatory changes. We aim to provide the kind of in-depth content that helps both newcomers and experienced traders make sense of the market.\n\nWe believe our focus on quality journalism would complement the resources you already provide. Thanks for your consideration!",
      "backlink_url": "https://coinography.com",
      "backlink_anchor_text": "Coinography (https://coinography.com)",
      "image_path": null,
      "submission_instructions": "Use the general contact or partnership email to suggest a link."

  ]
